revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] (National Learning) active from the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] to the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], [[Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]], [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and [[Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, [[:en:Kanda|Kanda]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (modern-day [[:en:Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the [[Oda clan]] genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the [[Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] and the [[Printing Bureau]] but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in [[Osaka]]. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association. == See Also == * [[:en:Genealogy|Genealogy]] * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[Samurai]] * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]
== External Links == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library's digital collection of Suzuki Motoshi's works]

}}

''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association. == See Also == * [[:en:Genealogy|Genealogy]] * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[Samurai]] * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]

revision.text:
'''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] (National Learning) active from the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] to the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]], [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and [[:en:Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, [[:en:Kanda|Kanda]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (modern-day [[:en:Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] and the [[:en:Printing Bureau|Printing Bureau]] but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association. == See Also == * [[:en:Genealogy|Genealogy]] * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[Samurai]] * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]
== External Links == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library's digital collection of Suzuki Motoshi's works]

'''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] (National Learning) active from the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] to the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]], [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and [[:en:Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, [[:en:Kanda|Kanda]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (modern-day [[:en:Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] and the [[:en:Printing Bureau|Printing Bureau]] but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.

Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.

Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] and the [[:en:Printing Bureau|Printing Bureau]] but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1831|death_date={{death date|1894|4|15}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q6536470]] Wikidata name: Suzuki Matoshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] (National Learning) active from the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] to the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]], [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and [[:en:Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, [[:en:Kanda|Kanda]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (modern-day [[:en:Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] and the [[:en:Printing Bureau|Printing Bureau]] but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association. == See Also == * [[:en:Genealogy|Genealogy]] * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]
== External Links == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library's digital collection of Suzuki Motoshi's works]

In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] and the [[:en:Printing Bureau|Printing Bureau]] but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.

''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1831|death_date={{death date|1894|4|15}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6536470]] Wikidata name: Suzuki Matoshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] (National Learning) active from the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] to the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]].
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]], [[:en:Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|Ministry of Education]], and [[:en:Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, [[:en:Kanda|Kanda]], [[:en:Edo|Edo]] (modern-day [[:en:Chiyoda, Tokyo|Chiyoda]], Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]] before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] and the [[:en:Printing Bureau|Printing Bureau]] but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association. == See Also == * [[:en:Genealogy|Genealogy]] * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]
== External Links == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library's digital collection of Suzuki Motoshi's works]

He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the [[:en:Kururi Domain|Kururi Domain]], and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]] genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the [[:en:Ministry of Justice (Japan)|Ministry of Justice]] and the [[:en:Printing Bureau|Printing Bureau]] but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].

His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.

Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.

He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in [[:en:Osaka|Osaka]]. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.

Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1831|death_date={{death date|1894|4|15}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q6536470]] Wikidata name: Suzuki Matoshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} (National Learning) active from the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice}}, {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|WD=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education}}, and [[:en:Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, {{ill|Kanda|en|Kanda|WD=Q415424|lt=Kanda}}, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} (modern-day {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda}}, Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}} before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}, and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan}} genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice}} and the {{ill|Printing Bureau|en|Printing Bureau|WD=Q10910309|lt=Printing Bureau}} but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association. == See Also == * {{ill|Genealogy|en|Genealogy|WD=Q47307|lt=Genealogy}} * {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} * {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1831|death_date={{death date|1894|4|15}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}}} '''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} (National Learning) active from the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}.
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice}}, {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|WD=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education}}, and [[:en:Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, {{ill|Kanda|en|Kanda|WD=Q415424|lt=Kanda}}, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} (modern-day {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda}}, Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto}} before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain}}, and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan}} genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice}} and the {{ill|Printing Bureau|en|Printing Bureau|WD=Q10910309|lt=Printing Bureau}} but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}}. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association. == See Also == * {{ill|Genealogy|en|Genealogy|WD=Q47307|lt=Genealogy}} * {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku}} * {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]

From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association.
== See Also == * {{ill|Genealogy|en|Genealogy|WD=Q47307|lt=Genealogy|1=Genealogy}} * {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} * {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鈴木眞年]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1831|death_date={{death date|1894|4|15}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}}} '''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} (National Learning) active from the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (Japan)}}, {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|WD=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, and [[:en:Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, {{ill|Kanda|en|Kanda|WD=Q415424|lt=Kanda|1=Kanda}}, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} (modern-day {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|WD=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|WD=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}} before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|WD=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain|1=Kururi Domain}}, and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan|1=Oda clan}} genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (Japan)}} and the {{ill|Printing Bureau|en|Printing Bureau|WD=Q10910309|lt=Printing Bureau|1=Printing Bureau}} but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association.

== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|qid=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}} before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|qid=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain|1=Kururi Domain}}, and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|qid=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan|1=Oda clan}} genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|qid=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (Japan)}} and the {{ill|Printing Bureau|en|Printing Bureau|qid=Q10910309|lt=Printing Bureau|1=Printing Bureau}} but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association.
== See Also == * {{ill|Genealogy|en|Genealogy|qid=Q47307|lt=Genealogy|1=Genealogy}} * {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} * {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} * [[Japanese Imperial Lineage]]
== External Links == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library's digital collection of Suzuki Motoshi's works]

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1831|death_date={{death date|1894|4|15}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}}} '''Suzuki Motoshi''' (鈴木 真年, also written as 鈴木 眞年, 1831 – April 15, 1894) was a Japanese genealogist and scholar of {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} (National Learning) active from the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}.
He initially served under the [[:en:Kishu-Tokugawa clan|Kishu-Tokugawa clan]], working on genealogical compilation projects. Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], he was employed by the [[Danjōdai]] (Bureau of Censors) and later worked for various government ministries, including the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]], {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|qid=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (Japan)}}, {{ill|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|en|Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture|qid=Q11080185|lt=Ministry of Education|1=Ministry of Education, Science, Sports and Culture}}, and [[:en:Ministry of the Army (Japan)|Ministry of the Army]]. Throughout his career, Suzuki compiled numerous genealogical collections, both official and private.
== Early Life == Suzuki was born in 1831 (Tenpō 2) in Kamakurabashi, {{ill|Kanda|en|Kanda|qid=Q415424|lt=Kanda|1=Kanda}}, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} (modern-day {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|qid=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, Tokyo), as the eldest son of Jinnosuke Suzuki, a tobacco merchant. Later, he adopted the name Motoshi.
In 1847, due to health issues, he retreated to [[Kumano Hongū Taisha]] in [[Kishu Province]] to recuperate. During this time, he wrote drafts of works such as "Study of Ancient Immigrants to Japan" and "Genealogy of the Three Tokugawa Branches."
== Career == In 1849, Suzuki returned to Edo and briefly married the daughter of a {{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|qid=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}} before divorcing shortly afterward. He relinquished his family's headship to his younger brother Hirokichi, moved to the mountains of [[:en:Kozuke Province|Kozuke Province]], and became a Buddhist monk under the name Fuzon.
In 1858, following his father's death, Suzuki left the monastic life, renamed himself Motoyoshi, and later joined the [[Ontakekyo]] sect. By 1860, he married Nobuko, the daughter of a physician in the {{ill|Kururi Domain|en|Kururi Domain|qid=Q1002840|lt=Kururi Domain|1=Kururi Domain}}, and was appointed as a senior instructor in Ontakekyo. Around this time, he began studying genealogy under Kurihara Nobumitsu, focusing on the genealogies of samurai families.
In 1865, Suzuki joined the [[:en:Wakayama Domain|Wakayama Domain]] and took charge of its genealogical records. Residing near the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] shrines, he contributed to related projects while producing genealogical manuscripts such as drafts of the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|qid=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan|1=Oda clan}} genealogy.
== Government Service == After the establishment of the [[Danjōdai]] in 1869, Suzuki resigned from the Wakayama Domain to work as a government official, earning a monthly salary of 50 yen. In 1871, he transferred to the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]] as an official. After leaving this post in 1873, he briefly served in the {{ill|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|en|Ministry of Justice (Japan)|qid=Q1031145|lt=Ministry of Justice|1=Ministry of Justice (Japan)}} and the {{ill|Printing Bureau|en|Printing Bureau|qid=Q10910309|lt=Printing Bureau|1=Printing Bureau}} but resigned due to health issues. From 1880 to 1885, he served in the Ministry of the Army's General Staff Office, working on military history projects.
Suzuki also contributed to academic projects, joining the Geological Society of Japan in 1882 and working on integrating genealogy and geographical studies. In 1888, he participated in the compilation of the "Chronological History of Japan" at the recommendation of [[Shigeno Yasutsugu]].
== Later Years and Death == After retiring from public service in 1891, Suzuki focused on establishing a Kokugaku school and assisting with the restoration of the [[Kumano Taisha]]. He passed away suddenly on April 15, 1894, at his residence in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}. He was 64 years old and was buried in [[:en:Zoshigaya Cemetery|Zoshigaya Cemetery]], Tokyo. His posthumous name was Matsubaku-in Raiyo Tenkyō Shinkū Koji.
== Works == Suzuki compiled and edited numerous genealogical records, including: * "Genealogies of 100 Families" (1871) * "Family Genealogies of Various Clans" (1875) * "Annotated Kojiki" (1888) * "New Genealogies of the Nitta Clan" (1891)
Some of his unpublished manuscripts, such as "Korean Dynastic Genealogies," remain preserved in libraries.
== References == <references />
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book |title=Suzuki Motoshi-den |author=Suzuki Boujin |publisher= |year=1943 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1874380/1/4 }} * [[Hoga Toshio]] (1986). ''Collected Genealogies of Ancient Clans.'' Ancient Clans Research Association.
